repository.corpname: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

did.abstract: Papers of Dr. Joyce Brothers include correspondence, subject files, letters received, scripts, clippings, contracts, awards, photographs, films, videos, and articles and books by Dr. Brothers.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Boxes 63-67 are closed for 90 years from date of creation.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Due to the fragility and potential degradation of moving image and sound recordings, viewing and listening is limited to items that have been digitized. You may request to have view the digitized version, or request to have the item digitized for access. Information on ordering access copies may be found on the Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections webpage. Audio visual material that has been digitized is noted in the finding aid. This restriction covers all film, all video, all tape recordings, and AV material in boxes 193-229, 240-244, and 250-251.

bioghist.head: BIOGRAPHICAL NOTE
bioghist.p: Joyce Diane Brothers (October 20, 1927 – May 13, 2013) was an American psychologist, television personality, advice columnist, and writer. She graduated from Cornell University in 1947, and with a PhD from Columbia University in 1953.

She first became famous in 1955 for winning the top prize on the American game show "The $64,000 Question." Her fame from the game show allowed her to go on to host various advice columns and television shows, which established her as a pioneer in the field of "pop (popular) psychology".

Brothers is often credited as the first to normalize psychological concepts to the American mainstream. Her syndicated columns were featured in newspapers and magazines, including a monthly column for Good Housekeeping, in which she contributed for nearly 40 years. Her television programs, where she read letters from viewers and offered advice, began in 1958.

As Brothers quickly became the "face of psychology" for American audiences, she often appeared in various television roles, usually as herself. From the 1970s onward, she also began to accept fictional roles that parodied her "woman psychologist" persona. She is noted for working continuously for five decades across various genres.

prefercite.head: Cite As:
prefercite.p: Dr. Joyce Brothers papers, #4254. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.

scopecontent.head: COLLECTION DESCRIPTION
scopecontent.p: Papers of Dr. Joyce Brothers include correspondence, subject files, scripts, clippings, contracts, awards, photographs, video, film, tape recordings, and books documenting the over 50 year career of Dr. Joyce Brothers.

custodhist.head: CUSTODIAL HISTORY
custodhist.p: The first donation was made in 1986 by Dr. Brothers, with subsequent donations being made yearly. Upon her death in 2013, the rest of her material, including her filing cabinets of research and articles, was donated by her family. Thirteen filing cabinets containing notes, articles, and other documents, were badly damaged by storage conditions and never accessioned by Cornell. That material was destroyed in 2020.
An additional 5 boxes of material was retrieved from the Brothers' farm house in 2023.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: These boxes were removed from filing cabinets. Boxes 14-17 were kept in their original order with original titles. Boxes 18-20 were compiled by the archivist from miscellaneous boxes and files. Some of the folder titles for those boxes were created by the archivist. The filing cabinets were originally labelled "Book publishing, media tours, promos, luce, book review, books-scripts, agents (various) #1"

Series VII. Television and Radio Programs by Brothers

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: "Consult Dr. Brothers" changed titles and is also referred to as "The Dr. Joyce Brothers Show" or "The Dr. Joyce Brothers Program" in the early years. It is unclear when exactly the name changed, but was clearly "Consult Dr. Brothers" in 1961. Documents that were not labelled with the show title were labelled as "Consult Dr. Brothers" for consistency. The show was picked up by NBC and later ABC (1961). It ran from 1959 to circa 1961 and preceded "Tell Me, Dr. Brothers".

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: These notes likely relate to Consult Dr. Brothers, as they were found with related material, but may also contain information on "Tell Me, Dr. Brothers." Included are notes for redaction and censorship for the show from WEWS.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Proposed program with Dr. Brothers dramatic presentation of cases based on actual letter. This show was 20-25 minutes. Brothers was represented by Lester Lewis Associates. This is a different show than "Consult Dr. Brothers"/"The Dr. Joyce Brothers show." It was not filmed and did not air, though a promo was shot.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Brothers possessed multiple sets of films of Tell Me, Dr. Brothers. The sets were labelled A, B, and C, though many canisters were unlabelled. The best copy of each episode was digitized, regardless of which set it came from. The dates on original canisters do not match the date of filming, but may be air dates or dates that a film copy was made.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Known as the "Tobies" (after Brothers' assistant), this card file consists of index cards with articles stapled to them. When asked to speak or right on a specific subject, Brothers' would pull from these cards as her references.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: These cards are organized by alphabetically by topic, and original order has been maintained. These cards were originally housed in 16 filing cabinets.
